quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Hydrostatic pressure"?

options.text: f' gives the topology (hills and valleys); f'' gives the shape (cupping up or down); together they determine the complete curve shape
= e^(f(x)) · f'(x); the most common chain rule application with exponentials
P = ρgh; pressure at depth h below the surface; ρ = fluid density, g = gravitational acceleration
At a critical point c: f' changes + to -: local max; f' changes - to +: local min; no sign change: neither

quiz_questions.explanation: "Hydrostatic pressure" is defined as: P = ρgh; pressure at depth h below the surface; ρ = fluid density, g = gravitational acceleration. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Force on a submerged plate"?

options.text: d/dx[cf(x)] = c·f'(x); constants factor out of the derivative
F(x) = ∫ₐˣ f(t)dt; represents the accumulated area from a to x; its derivative is f(x) by FTC Part 1
F = ∫₀ᴴ ρg·w(h)·h dh; integrates pressure × width over the depth range of the plate
Finding a specific antiderivative by using a condition like F(0) = 3 to determine the value of C

quiz_questions.explanation: "Force on a submerged plate" is defined as: F = ∫₀ᴴ ρg·w(h)·h dh; integrates pressure × width over the depth range of the plate. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Horizontal strip method"?

options.text: Uses the midpoint of each subinterval: M_n = Σf((xᵢ + xᵢ₊₁)/2)Δx; generally more accurate than left/right
Divide the plate into thin horizontal strips of width dh; each strip has approximately constant pressure ρgh and area w(h)·dh
d/dx[xⁿ] = nxⁿ⁻¹ for any real n; includes x⁻¹ = 1/x, x^(1/2) = √x, x^(2/3), etc.
At a critical point c: f' changes + to -: local max; f' changes - to +: local min; no sign change: neither

quiz_questions.explanation: "Horizontal strip method" is defined as: Divide the plate into thin horizontal strips of width dh; each strip has approximately constant pressure ρgh and area w(h)·dh. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Coordinate system setup"?

options.text: Find dy/dt given dx/dt (or vice versa) by differentiating an equation relating x and y with respect to time t
μ = E[X] = ∫₋∞^∞ x·f(x)dx; the 'center of mass' of the probability distribution
The derivative of the natural log; note that d/dx[ln|x|] = 1/x for x ≠ 0
Choose h measuring depth down from the surface (h = 0 at surface); or set up y-axis and convert

quiz_questions.explanation: "Coordinate system setup" is defined as: Choose h measuring depth down from the surface (h = 0 at surface); or set up y-axis and convert. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: In the context of Fluid Pressure and Force, which best defines "Units"?

options.text: ∫f'(x)/f(x)dx = ln|f(x)| + C (u = f(x)); ∫f'(x)·[f(x)]ⁿdx = [f(x)]ⁿ⁺¹/(n+1) + C
Pressure in pascals (Pa = N/m²) or lb/ft²; force in newtons (N) or pounds (lb)
If f'(c) = 0 and f''(c) > 0: local minimum (concave up); f''(c) < 0: local maximum (concave down)
d/dx[f(x) ± g(x)] = f'(x) ± g'(x); derivative of a sum/difference is the sum/difference of derivatives

quiz_questions.explanation: In Fluid Pressure and Force, "Units" refers to: Pressure in pascals (Pa = N/m²) or lb/ft²; force in newtons (N) or pounds (lb).

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "P = ρgh; pressure at depth h below the surface; ρ = fluid density, g = gravitational acceleration"?

options.text: Notation
Hydrostatic pressure
One-sided limits
Justification language

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Hydrostatic pressure".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "F = ∫₀ᴴ ρg·w(h)·h dh; integrates pressure × width over the depth range of the plate"?

options.text: Force on a submerged plate
Left/Right/Midpoint sum
Fractional exponent derivatives

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Divide the plate into thin horizontal strips of width dh; each strip has approximately constant pressure ρgh and area w(h)·dh"?

options.text: Verification technique
d/dx[ln x] = 1/x
Horizontal strip method
Acceleration

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Horizontal strip method".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Choose h measuring depth down from the surface (h = 0 at surface); or set up y-axis and convert"?

options.text: Shadow problems
Restricting domain
Coordinate system setup
d/dx[f(x)^g(x)]

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Coordinate system setup".

quiz_questions.question_text: In Fluid Pressure and Force, which term matches: "Pressure in pascals (Pa = N/m²) or lb/ft²; force in newtons (N) or pounds (lb)"?

options.text: Key points to plot
Notation
Removable discontinuity
Units

quiz_questions.explanation: In Fluid Pressure and Force, this refers to "Units".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Fluid Pressure and Force is accurate?

options.text: The inflection point occurs at P = K/2, where the population is growing fastest.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Fluid Pressure and Force as covered in this lesson.

options.text: Visualizing MVT helps interpret derivative graphs and connect graphical and analytical representations.

Hydrostatic pressure at depth h: P = ρgh (in Pa or lb/ft²).
After integrating in terms of u, substitute back: u = g(x).

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Fluid Pressure and Force as covered in this lesson.

Limits and Continuity are the foundation of calculus.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Fluid Pressure and Force?

options.text: The indefinite integral ∫f(x)dx = F(x) + C, where C is the constant of integration (since derivatives of constants are zero).
The key insight is that pressure increases with depth, so different parts of the plate experience different pressures.
Arc length of y = f(x) from a to b: L = ∫ₐᵇ √(1 + [f'(x)]²)dx.
If f'(x) > 0 on an interval, then f is increasing on that interval; if f'(x) < 0, f is decreasing.

options.text: Implicit Differentiation finds dy/dx when y is not explicitly solved as a function of x.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Fluid Pressure and Force?

options.text: Long-term drug concentration models: horizontal asymptotes represent the steady-state drug level that the body approaches with regular dosing
Dam design: engineers compute total hydrostatic force on dam faces to determine required structural strength and materials
Computer-aided design (CAD): finding normal vectors to complex curved surfaces defined implicitly requires combined differentiation techniques
Hydrology: ∫(inflow rate - outflow rate)dt gives the net change in reservoir water level over a drought period

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Fluid Pressure and Force.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Fluid Pressure and Force?

options.text: Electrical engineering: integrating sinusoidal voltage V(t) = V₀sin(ωt) across a capacitor to find charge: Q = ∫V dt = -(V₀/ω)cos(ωt) + C
Planetary orbits: elliptical orbits x²/a² + y²/b² = 1 analyzed with implicit differentiation to find orbital velocity direction at each point
Submarine hull design: calculating pressure at operating depth determines hull thickness needed to prevent collapse
Population dynamics: given a birth rate minus death rate function, calculate total population change over a decade

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Fluid Pressure and Force.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Fluid Pressure and Force applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Water tank problems: given inflow rate and outflow rate functions, compute water level at any time t and find when the tank is fullest
Coulomb's law: electric force F = kq₁q₂/r² → ∞ as distance r → 0, creating a vertical asymptote that reflects the impossibility of zero separation between charges
Aquarium design: the total force on viewing windows increases dramatically with depth, requiring thicker glass for deeper tanks
AP exam problems frequently ask students to apply MVT to piecewise data: 'Given the table, must there be a value c where f'(c) = 3?'

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Fluid Pressure and Force.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Fluid Pressure and Force?

options.text: Electrical engineering: integrating sinusoidal voltage V(t) = V₀sin(ωt) across a capacitor to find charge: Q = ∫V dt = -(V₀/ω)cos(ωt) + C
AP exam problems frequently ask students to apply MVT to piecewise data: 'Given the table, must there be a value c where f'(c) = 3?'
Acceleration as the second derivative of position: a car speeding up has positive s''(t), slowing down has negative s''(t), and constant speed has s''(t) = 0
Dam design: engineers compute total hydrostatic force on dam faces to determine required structural strength and materials

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Fluid Pressure and Force.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Fluid Pressure and Force in practice?

options.text: Submarine hull design: calculating pressure at operating depth determines hull thickness needed to prevent collapse
Air traffic control: two planes approaching an intersection at different speeds—related rates using the Pythagorean theorem to find the rate of distance change
Orbital mechanics: computing orbital periods and trajectories involves integrals with expressions like √(a²-x²), solved with trigonometric substitution
Planetary orbits: elliptical orbits x²/a² + y²/b² = 1 analyzed with implicit differentiation to find orbital velocity direction at each point

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Fluid Pressure and Force.
